description: Kurdish militant political organization and armed guerrilla group primarily based in mountainous Kurdish-majority regions of southeastern Turkey, northern Iraq, and northeastern Syria. Founded on 27 November 1978 in Ziyaret, Lice, by Abdullah Öcalan and others. Initially sought an independent Kurdish state, later shifted to seeking autonomy and cultural rights within Turkey. Designated as a terrorist organization by Turkey, the US, EU, and others. Declared a ceasefire in March 2025 and announced plans for dissolution.

country_links.evidence_quote: After harboring Ali Riza Altun, Austria arranged a flight to Iraq for him
country_links.context: Austria arranged a flight to Iraq for a PKK leader, facilitating his movement.

country_links.evidence_quote: It was reported in 2004 that there was another political training camp in Belgium
country_links.context: The PKK had a political training camp in Belgium.

country_links.evidence_quote: The Republic of Cyprus has been instrumental in helping Greece supply arms to the PKK.
country_links.context: The Republic of Cyprus helped Greece supply arms to the PKK.

country_links.evidence_quote: From 1984, the PKK became a paramilitary group with training camps in Turkish Kurdistan, Iraqi Kurdistan, Syria, Lebanon and France.
country_links.context: The PKK had training camps in France.

country_links.evidence_quote: In 1983, the Association of Artists ( Hunerkom [ ku ] ) was established in Germany under the lead of the music group Koma Berxwedan [ ku ]
country_links.context: The PKK established its cultural branch Hunerkom in Germany and has a presence in mosques there.

country_links.evidence_quote: Greece has for years supported the PKK movement. They even gave us arms and rockets. Greek officers gave guerrilla training and explosives training to our militants
country_links.context: The PKK received training and arms from Greece, with Greek officers providing guerrilla and explosives training at a camp in Lavrion.

country_links.evidence_quote: The PKK received significant support from the Syrian government, which allowed it to maintain headquarters in Damascus, as well as some support from the governments of Iran, Iraq, and Libya.
country_links.context: The PKK received support from the government of Iran.

country_links.evidence_quote: recruited new fighters mainly from Turkey, but also from Syria, Iraq, Iran, and Western countries... 3,000 to 3,500 are located in northern Iraq.
country_links.context: The PKK recruited fighters from Iraq and has a significant presence in northern Iraq.

country_links.evidence_quote: From 1984, the PKK became a paramilitary group with training camps in Turkish Kurdistan, Iraqi Kurdistan, Syria, Lebanon and France.
country_links.context: The PKK had training camps in Lebanon and held a press conference in Bar Elias.

country_links.evidence_quote: The PKK received significant support from the Syrian government, which allowed it to maintain headquarters in Damascus, as well as some support from the governments of Iran, Iraq, and Libya.
country_links.context: The PKK received support from the government of Libya.

country_links.evidence_quote: Its activities spread over Kurdish community centers in France , Germany and the Netherlands .
country_links.context: The PKK's cultural branch Hunerkom spread activities over Kurdish community centers in the Netherlands.

country_links.evidence_quote: Switzerland has taken its own measures to monitor and restrict the group's activities on Swiss soil, including banning the collection of funds for the group
country_links.context: The PKK had activities on Swiss soil, leading Switzerland to ban fund collection for the group.

country_links.evidence_quote: recruited new fighters mainly from Turkey, but also from Syria, Iraq, Iran, and Western countries... Syria had provided valuable safe havens to PKK in the region of Beqaa Valley.
country_links.context: The PKK recruited fighters from Syria and received safe havens and training in Syria's Beqaa Valley.

country_links.evidence_quote: British police arrested seven people in London as part of a counter-terrorism investigation linked to the PKK.
country_links.context: British police arrested seven people in London linked to the PKK and searched locations including the Kurdish Community Centre.
